I wasn’t even planning to dig into this. But when I saw Senator Chiz Escudero’s 2024 SALN showing a P18.84 million, something didn’t sit right. Especially since his wife, Heart Evangelista, has been openly flaunting a lifestyle worth hundreds of millions. Now, I’m not claiming to be an expert on SALNs or finance. I’m just someone who reads, observes, and asks the questions that common sense demands. So, I got curious—as usual—and decided to look into it. How could the “poorest senator” afford million-dollar gifts, designer collections, and a life that looks like it belongs on a Forbes list?

## Understanding What a SALN Really Means

Before we get to the gifts and the math, let’s slow down for a second and understand what a Statement of Assets, Liabilities, and Net Worth (SALN) actually represents. Every government official, from senators to city clerks, is required by law to file one. It’s supposed to be a window into their financial life—how much they own, how much they owe, and how their wealth changes over time. The intent is simple: transparency. But in practice, it’s closer to an honor system. Officials list their properties, cash, vehicles, investments, and even jewelry, along with their spouse’s and children’s. The problem? There’s no built-in mechanism to verify if what they declare is complete or accurate. Unless someone files a formal complaint or an investigation begins, no one checks if those numbers actually reflect reality. That’s where things get tricky. A property worth fifty million can appear as five million on paper if declared at its original purchase cost. A car can “disappear” from the list if it’s suddenly “sold” to a relative. And luxury items gifted to a spouse—well, those can easily fall through the cracks if treated as personal rather than conjugal property. This system depends on honesty. And honesty, in politics, is often negotiable.

## Chiz Escudero’s Declared Wealth vs. His Lifestyle

Chiz Escudero’s 2024 SALN places his net worth at P18.84 million, officially making him the “poorest senator.” It’s a title he’s worn before. In 2015, his net worth was listed at P5.8 million, and in 2018, about P8.5 million. The numbers grew, but not by much—certainly not enough to match the life he and Heart Evangelista lead in public. His 2024 declaration includes a few real properties reportedly acquired through inheritance back in the early 2000s, some cash in the bank, and personal properties. But that’s where curiosity starts to bite. Because while the numbers on the document stay small, the items on display grow bigger every year.

## The Gifts That Don’t Add Up

Let’s talk about the numbers that caught everyone’s attention. The kind that sparkle, weigh a few carats, and come in orange boxes. The most striking example is the Paraiba tourmaline ring Chiz supposedly gave Heart for her birthday in 2024. Reports and jewelry experts pegged it at around ₱56 million—roughly three times his entire declared net worth. It’s a six-carat gemstone, the kind rarely seen outside private auctions or museum collections. Then there’s the Hermès collection. Heart has several, but one stands out: the Himalaya Birkin, often called the “holy grail” of handbags. Its auction value sits at around ₱21 million, depending on condition and year. And that’s just one bag. She owns multiple Birkins and Kellys—some painted, some exotic, all expensive. In one photo alone, she was seen carrying pieces that experts estimated at over ₱20 million combined. These aren’t the kind of bags you buy on impulse. These are investment pieces reserved for people whose net worth has many more zeros than what Chiz’s SALN suggests. What makes this even harder to ignore is how Heart spends beyond herself. In early 2025, she gifted her entire glam team with Hermès bags as a token of appreciation. A move like that, for a team of several people, easily costs ₱10 to ₱15 million. And when she says she wants her team to have what she has, you can almost admire the generosity. But for the average Filipino, the question remains: where does that kind of liquidity come from?

## The Jewelry, Fashion, and Travel Lifestyle

Heart Evangelista’s love for jewelry isn’t exactly a secret. She’s been seen wearing pieces that could fund small towns. Take her Panthère de Cartier necklace, a white gold piece set with emeralds, onyx, and over a thousand diamonds. Cartier even hosted a private event in Hong Kong just for her. Then there’s her Van Cleef collection, which her makeup artist casually mentioned on social media. Add to that several Bvlgari pieces, including a Serpenti necklace that could easily be valued between ₱20 to ₱50 million—and that’s before we even touch her designer wardrobe. Her stylist once claimed that Heart was “the second biggest buyer of Saint Laurent in the world.” Not in Manila. In the world. And then there’s Paris Fashion Week—the crown jewel of her public image. She’s admitted earning around ₱2 million per show for some appearances. But the math doesn’t sit comfortably. Flights, hotels, glam team, outfits, production, and photography for a multi-week stay in Europe easily run between ₱5 to ₱10 million per trip. Multiply that by multiple seasons a year, and the numbers just don’t line up neatly with the life of a public servant’s wife whose husband’s total declared wealth is less than the cost of one trip. At this point, it’s less about what she wears and more about what it reveals. Because even without knowing the exact price tags, anyone can see that this isn’t a P18-million lifestyle. This is a billionaire’s playground.

## The Income Question: Can Heart Afford This Herself?

This is where things start to get interesting. Heart Evangelista often says she works hard for everything she has. She has dozens of endorsements, both local and international—GCash, Cream Silk, Ponds, Avon, SM, Purefoods, and more. She’s a brand unto herself. However, several investigative reports and social media watchdogs have pointed out that she’s never appeared on the Bureau of Internal Revenue’s list of top celebrity taxpayers. Those lists often include Vice Ganda, Kathryn Bernardo, Daniel Padilla, and other big earners who openly declare their high incomes and pay accordingly. Some say she earns millions per fashion week appearance, and that may be true. But even if we assume she earns ₱100 million a year, her spending on jewelry, gifts, and couture still seems to outpace her visible income. When pressed about her wealth, she often points to her “27 years of hard work.” That’s fair—but even 27 years of hard work at the top level doesn’t explain a ₱56-million ring appearing out of nowhere as a gift from a “poor” senator. The question remains: if the luxury isn’t fully funded by her career, then where is the extra money coming from?

## The Senator’s Salary: Follow the Money

Let’s strip it down to the basics. A senator’s salary in the Philippines ranges between ₱90,000 and ₱293,000 per month, depending on their grade and tenure. Even at the top bracket, that’s roughly ₱3.5 million a year. Not bad by ordinary standards, but nowhere near enough to buy million-peso jewelry or fund multiple luxury trips abroad. Of course, that’s just the base. Senators also earn more from committee chairmanships, representation, and travel allowances. Some estimates put their total monthly take-home, including various allowances, at around ₱5 million. Multiply that by twelve, and you get ₱60 million a year. That sounds substantial until you remember that one Paraiba ring alone costs ₱56 million. And that’s just one item. His wife’s wardrobe and accessories could easily push past ₱100 million in total value. Then there’s the timing. In September 2025, Chiz was ousted as Senate President amid corruption allegations and a drop in public trust. He was linked to Maynard Ngu, the CEO of Cherry Mobile, who was accused of acting as his “bagman” for DPWH flood control projects—the same projects now under scrutiny for billions in suspected kickbacks. Witnesses described bags of cash being delivered to high-ranking officials. Chiz, of course, denied everything. Before that controversy, Chiz even called for lifestyle checks among public officials, quoting the law that requires them to “lead modest lives.” On social media, people were quick to point out the irony. When the math looks that off, people don’t need to be accountants to see the imbalance. They just need to look at the photos.

## The SALN Loopholes: How Wealth Disappears from Paper

There are simple, obvious ways that big money doesn't show up in a SALN. None of these are secrets; they’re just standard maneuvers for people who want to stay rich while looking modest on paper.
1. Declare at purchase price. A house bought years ago can stay listed at acquisition cost while its market value has quadrupled.
2. Gifts that “aren’t yours.” Spouses and close associates can treat luxury items as personal gifts, letting them stay off the conjugal asset list.
3. Use other names. Companies, relatives, friends, shell entities—put assets under third parties and keep them off the individual’s declaration.
4. Offshore and cross-border purchases. Items bought or stored abroad, or funds parked in foreign accounts, are notoriously hard to track.
5. Timing tricks. Move assets before the filing deadline. Transfer titles. “Sell” something to a relative temporarily.
6. Undervalue jewellery and vehicles. Declare a car at a conservative price. List jewelry at an appraised value that is significantly lower than retail.
7. Weak verification. The system largely runs on declarations. Unless a complaint is filed, or an audit is triggered, most SALNs are never checked.
8. Legal fine-print. Prenups, separate-property claims, or creative contract language can be used to argue that assets belong solely to the spouse.
When you stack those moves together, a senator’s P18.8 million on paper and a P100-million lifestyle can exist at the same time. The law is there, but the enforcement is missing.

## Public Reaction: The Court of Public Opinion

When the SALN figures came out, the public didn’t just scroll past them. Reddit threads, Facebook groups, and TikTok explainers went viral. Many pointed out the obvious inconsistency: how could the “poorest senator” afford a ₱56-million ring when his declared wealth doesn’t even reach ₱20 million? Others questioned why there’s still no lifestyle check happening, especially after his ousting as Senate President. The reactions weren’t just gossip. They came from frustration. Filipinos are dealing with rising prices, low wages, and a government that constantly asks them to sacrifice. Seeing a public official and his wife flaunt more wealth in one Instagram post than an entire community earns in a year feels like an insult. Heart tried to address the issue in a livestream, saying she’s been working hard for 27 years and doesn’t need anyone’s money. But in politics, it’s not just about what you earn; it’s about what you can prove. At this point, the court of public opinion has already delivered its verdict: the math doesn’t work, and until someone checks it, people will keep asking why.

## The Bottom Line: Questions That Demand Answers

After all the numbers, brands, and statements, it comes down to one thing: accountability. Chiz Escudero’s 2024 SALN shows ₱18.84 million in declared net worth. Yet the life we see—rings, handbags, and a lifestyle built on couture and privilege—tells a very different story. Even the most generous interpretation still leaves hundreds of millions unaccounted for. Heart Evangelista, on her part, has every right to enjoy success. But when her wealth and spending raise more questions than answers—and when those questions involve public money—the public has every reason to ask. The law requires transparency for a reason. Whether this is a matter of creative accounting, under-declaration, or something else entirely, the inconsistency is too big to ignore. A single ring shouldn’t be worth three times a senator’s entire declared wealth. A family’s lifestyle shouldn’t look this far removed from their paperwork. For once, it would be good to see a lifestyle check that doesn’t stop at headlines. Because when the poorest senator lives like the richest, it’s not just gossip anymore—it’s a question of trust, and the people deserve a clear answer.

Topic Request: What’s the Story Behind the ₱142.7 Billion Budget Insertion?

A follower asked me what the story is behind the alleged budget insertion tied to Senate President Francis "Chiz" Escudero. Here’s what we know so far—everything below is based on public records, media coverage, and official documents. No accusations here. Just verified facts and context.

Context: 2025 National Budget and Escudero’s Role

In December 2024, Congress finalized the ₱6.326-trillion General Appropriations Act (GAA) for 2025. This followed the proposal of a ₱6.352-trillion National Expenditure Program (NEP) submitted by Malacañang on July 29, 2024 (DBM, DOF). Senate President Francis Escudero, elected on May 20, 2024, signed the bicameral conference committee report that reconciled the House and Senate versions of the budget. The report was ratified by Congress on December 11, 2024, and signed into law by President Marcos on December 30, 2024 (PhilStar).

What’s Being Questioned: ₱142.7 Billion in Budget Insertions

A Vera Files report flagged a total of ₱142.7 billion in insertions allegedly introduced during the bicam period. A 103-page bicam summary document obtained by media outlets included specific funding allocations to selected provinces. A detailed list published by Politiko shows top allocations going to:
- Bulacan – ₱12.08 billion
- Sorsogon – ₱9.14 billion
- Davao Region – ₱7.21 billion

The documents cite project labels such as “Construction of Flood Mitigation Structures – Nationwide,” many of which lacked engineering specifics. Several entries used round-number allocations, and some were split into smaller projects—features similar to those flagged in past pork barrel misuse cases.

The BARMM Comparison

For comparison, the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao (BARMM) received a ₱94.41 billion budget for 2025 (Bangsamoro Parliament). According to the Philippine Statistics Authority, 34.8% of families in BARMM live below the poverty threshold based on the 2023 Family Income and Expenditure Survey (PSA).

Budget Cuts Elsewhere

While the ₱142.7 billion in insertions were introduced, other departments saw significant budget reductions:
The Department of Education (DepEd) experienced a ₱12 billion overall cut, of which ₱10 billion came from its computerization and ICT modernization programs, leaving ₱8 billion in the final budget for those items (PhilStar, GMA News).

Legal and Constitutional Questions

On January 29, 2025, a petition was filed with the Supreme Court challenging the constitutionality of the 2025 budget, citing violations of:
- Article VI, Section 25(1) – Improper augmentation of appropriations
- Article XIV, Section 5(5) – Prioritization of education funding
- Article II, Section 15 – The right to health
- Article VI, Section 27 – Requirement for itemized appropriations

On February 4, 2025, the Court docketed the case and directed the House, Senate, and Executive to respond within ten days (Manila Bulletin). Media reports also confirmed that certain bicam documents included blank budget line items that were filled in after ratification—raising concerns over procedural violations (BusinessWorld).

Presidential Vetoes

President Marcos vetoed ₱194 billion worth of items in the 2025 budget:
- ₱168.24 billion in unprogrammed appropriations
- ₱26.065 billion from the Department of Public Works and Highways (DPWH)

However, the veto message did not cite any of the ₱142.7 billion in insertions published by the media (PCO).

Senate Support and Bloc Alignments

During the budget approval and leadership transition, 13 to 18 senators were reported to have supported Escudero’s Senate presidency. These figures come from media interviews and document lists tracking the leadership change (Inquirer, Politiko). While formal “whip” positions exist in the Senate, vote consolidation tends to operate more informally through bloc memberships and shared regional interests.

Historical Context

The Priority Development Assistance Fund (PDAF) scandal involving Janet Napoles, exposed in 2013, siphoned an estimated ₱10 billion in government funds. This led to a Supreme Court ruling declaring PDAF unconstitutional (Wikipedia). In June 2024, Escudero stated in a GMA News interview that the 2025 budget process would be “transparent” and “free from questionable insertions.”

What Happens Next

As of July 23, 2025, the Supreme Court is reviewing multiple petitions challenging the budget’s legality. No formal investigations have been launched by the Senate Blue Ribbon Committee or the Department of Justice, although former Senate President Vicente Sotto III publicly called for a Blue Ribbon probe on July 21, 2025 (Manila Standard). The Commission on Audit (COA) has not released any audit observations related to the 2025 budget as disbursements only began in January.

It’s also worth noting that while Vera Files and Politiko cite official bicam documents, these files have not yet been publicly hosted, and should be treated as provisional until COA or DBM releases official tables.

## How the DDS Contribute to the Ruin of the Philippines—and Why It Still Matters

We didn’t plan this trip.
It was just after eight in the evening when we found out my wife had a seminar in Baguio. By eleven, we were in the car, coffee in the cup holders, the toddler asleep in the back, and the city lights fading behind us.

We got here around midnight. My wife’s now off to her seminar, my daughter’s still buried under the blankets, and I’m sitting on the balcony with a mug that’s already half-empty.

The mountains outside are still wrapped in fog. The air smells like pine and cold stone. It should be quiet. It should be peaceful.

And just like that, I’m pulled back into the noise.
Same threads. Same people. Same fury.

Diehard Duterte Supporters. DDS. Loud. Loyal. Unapologetically divisive. The kind of online presence that doesn’t just participate in a conversation—it suffocates it.

I didn’t come here to think about them. But how can I not, when their voice—this unrelenting noise—is part of the very air we breathe in the Philippines now?

So here I am, coffee in hand, wondering quietly:
How did we let it come to this?
And what kind of country will my daughter inherit if we keep pretending this is normal?

## The Illusion of Peace in a Noisy Republic

There’s something about Baguio mornings that slows everything down. Maybe it’s the air, or the way the fog rolls in like it’s trying to muffle the world for just a little longer. Even the clinking of plates at the buffet feels gentler somehow, like everyone’s still waking up on their own terms.

I wish the internet worked the same way.
But it doesn’t.
And it never sleeps.

I opened my feed just to kill time. A bit of scrolling, maybe a few dad jokes or throwback photos from friends. But no. There they were again—front and center. The DDS. Arguing. Attacking. Justifying. Always justifying.

It’s almost poetic how predictable they’ve become.
Any criticism of Duterte? “Yellowtard ka.”
A story about extrajudicial killings? “Eh kasi, adik.”
Someone asking for accountability? “Bayaran yan ng Dilawan.”

The rhythm of their defense is robotic now—scripted, shallow, cruel. And still, it works. It still dominates the conversation. It still drowns out everything else. The longer I watched the thread unfold, the more I felt that familiar tightness in my chest. Not from outrage—I’ve moved past that—but from fatigue.

You start wondering:
Is this the new national tone?
Are we really okay with this being the way we talk, the way we think?

I looked out the window again. The fog hadn’t cleared. The silence outside remained untouched. And that contrast—it hit hard. I’m in one of the quietest cities in the country, surrounded by mountains and pine trees, and still, the loudest thing in my life right now is a thread full of strangers shouting into a void.

And for some reason, I kept reading.
Maybe it was cathartic in a twisted way—to watch how bad it’s gotten, to confront just how far we’ve fallen. Or maybe I just needed a reminder that silence, real silence, is now a luxury in the Philippines. A privilege.

Because this isn’t just noise anymore. It’s not just angry posts or misguided loyalty. It’s deliberate. Designed. Sustained. And it’s changing us.

It’s shaping how we think.
How we vote.
What we tolerate.

And maybe it’s time we stop treating it like background noise—because the damage it’s doing is anything but quiet.

## What DDS Really Means

It always starts the same way.
You question a policy, and they call you bitter.
You bring up human rights, and they say you’re protecting criminals.
You quote the law, and they answer with a meme.

This reflection is nothing new. It’s been said many times.
The DDS aren’t just supporters—they’re a wall. One that’s been built brick by brick with blind loyalty, misinformation, and a refusal to look at the blood on the floor.

And let’s not pretend that wall isn’t dangerous.
The term **Davao Death Squad**—the alleged vigilante group tied to Duterte’s years as Davao mayor ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diehard_Duterte_Supporters)). That alone should’ve made people pause. But instead, it became a badge of honor, a sticker on bumpers, a hashtag used by millions.

We’ve grown so numb to violence, we don’t even flinch at naming a movement after murder.

But this kind of blind loyalty isn’t unique to them.
The Marcos loyalists of the past showed a similar kind of unyielding devotion. Even after the atrocities of Martial Law, their commitment remained unshaken, eventually paving the way for a return to power that many thought impossible.

And yes—if we’re being honest—even some Kakampinks mirrored the same behavior. Not all, but enough to create echo chambers of their own. They had their own walls, their own versions of the truth, their own way of dismissing anyone who didn’t fit their vision.

Still, let’s be real.
Put a Marcos loyalist, a Kakampink, and a DDS side by side—and nothing beats the destructive force of a DDS swarm.
Not in volume.
Not in vitriol.
Not in damage done.

And that’s what scares me.
Because in one of my older blogs, I wrote something I’ve been hesitant to say out loud again: that maybe, deep down, the DDS represents the darkest parts of the Filipino soul. The part that values strength over law. The part that would rather be led by a bully than by the truth.

And all it took was the garbage fire of Rodrigo Roa Duterte to drag it out into the open.
That’s the fear.
But the hope? It comes from the same place.

Because in the same blog, I also said this: the Kakampinks proved something else—that there’s still grace, wit, and heart in this country. That there’s a version of the Philippines that doesn’t need to shout to be heard.

And that, I hold on to.

## Trolls, Trolls, Trolls – How DDS Engineered the Noise

There was a time when online arguments were just that—arguments.
Messy, chaotic, often pointless. But still organic. Still human.
Then came the DDS.
And everything changed.

They didn’t just enter the conversation—they **took it over**.
They didn’t just reply—they **swarmed**.
And they didn’t just share their thoughts—they **manufactured a reality** and made sure it was the loudest thing on your feed.

By the numbers, their reach is staggering.
The DDS digital army was estimated to have **200,000 to 250,000 active profiles**, but only **30% were real people**. The rest? **Paid trolls**—fake names, fake photos, real impact ([Scimatic](https://scimatic.org/storage/journals/11/pdfs/3459.pdf)).

This wasn’t a movement. This was an operation.
At first, it was easy to laugh off. Their posts were clumsy, often full of typos and ALL CAPS RAGE. But while we were laughing, they were learning.

By the time the 2019 elections came around, their strategy had shifted. No more just shouting in the comments. They were now **microtargeting** users with personalized content, launching **multi-platform campaigns**, and inserting themselves in every political conversation that mattered ([New Mandala](https://www.newmandala.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/08/Digital-Disinformation-2019-Midterms.pdf)).

And their platform of choice? **Facebook.**
They didn’t bother with the noise of Twitter. They didn’t need the vanity of Instagram. Facebook gave them what they needed most: reach, reaction, and algorithmic reward. The more people engaged—out of anger, agreement, or disbelief—the more Facebook pushed their content. They turned the algorithm into a weapon ([Bloomberg](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2017-12-07/how-rodrigo-duterte-turned-facebook-into-a-weapon-with-a-little-help-from-facebook)).

They created echo chambers. Turned communities into warzones.
And every time you tried to speak sense into a thread, **you weren’t just debating someone**—you were stepping into a machine that existed to wear you down, not hear you out.

But it wasn’t just about pushing Duterte.
They rewrote events. They twisted facts. They branded activists, journalists, and even grieving mothers as enemies of the state.

They didn’t just push propaganda. They performed **mass memory manipulation**.
And slowly, it worked.
People began to doubt what they knew.
To question the very idea of truth.
To accept the idea that anger is stronger than evidence, and loyalty more important than law.

This isn’t noise anymore.
This is war—fought with memes instead of bullets, with emojis instead of ballots.
And like all wars, it has casualties.
When the trolls win, the nation loses.

## Manufacturing Consent for Murder

The DDS didn’t just defend Duterte online.
They prepared the stage for murder.
Long before the first body hit the pavement, the groundwork had already been laid—through hashtags, through comments, through a relentless campaign to dehumanize anyone labeled a "drug addict."

They didn’t kill with guns. They killed with words. And those words made it easier for others to pull the trigger.

The propaganda was simple:
Drug addicts are monsters. Criminals don’t deserve rights. If you want peace, you need to break a few bodies.

It was repeated so many times, so loudly, so aggressively, that people stopped questioning it. They stopped seeing people and started seeing statistics—or worse, trash.

And then the killings began.
Thousands dead.
Many of them poor. Many of them unnamed.
Some of them teenagers. Some of them already on their knees.

Official numbers tried to downplay it. But organizations on the ground knew better—**half of those deaths were carried out by vigilantes**, and many of those vigilantes operated with **clear police coordination** ([ACLED](https://acleddata.com/2018/10/18/dutertes-war-drug-related-violence-in-the-philippines/), [Human Rights Watch](https://www.hrw.org/report/2017/03/02/license-kill/philippine-police-killings-dutertes-war-drugs)).

It wasn’t chaos. It was policy.
And what did the DDS do while this was happening?
They cheered.

Their pages lit up with justifications:
“Eh kasi, adik.”
“Tama lang yan.”
“Ganyan ang disiplina.”

But it didn’t stop at justifications. They **celebrated** it.
They posted memes with kill counts. Laughed at grieving families. Flooded timelines with photos of bodies wrapped in packing tape, accompanied by laughing emojis. This wasn't just support; it was **moral disengagement** on a national scale ([Scimatic](https://scimatic.org/storage/journals/11/pdfs/3459.pdf)).

That’s when I realized something had shifted.
It wasn’t just about politics anymore. This was a **cultural collapse**.
We stopped flinching.
Stopped asking questions.
Started believing that violence equals order. That fear equals progress. That if you’re not doing anything wrong, you have nothing to fear—even as the bodies piled up next door.

Due process became a luxury.
Empathy became weakness.
And the body count became background noise.

And we let it happen.
We watched it unfold—on our feeds, in the news, on street corners. We saw the blood, read the names, and then we scrolled to the next post.
And the scariest part?
We didn’t even realize it was happening.
Until it already had.

## The Silencing of Civil Society

In a healthy democracy, critics keep the system honest.
Under Duterte, critics became enemies.
And the DDS? They made sure of it.

It wasn’t enough to justify killings. They needed to silence those who dared to speak.
Journalists, human rights defenders, students, lawyers, church groups—anyone who asked questions was painted as dangerous. Or ungrateful. Or worse, part of a conspiracy.

Discredit. Delegitimize. Destroy.
That was the playbook.
And it worked.

Online, DDS pages regularly branded critics as “bayaran,” “pabibo,” or “NPA sympathizers.” It wasn’t debate. It was demolition. They didn’t argue points. They **attacked reputations**.

Red-tagging became the new national sport.
And the DDS amplified it, turning government press conferences and tabloid headlines into viral memes—complete with Photoshop edits and comment threads dripping with threats.

It wasn’t just rhetoric.
It turned into real-life danger.
Activists were followed. Professors were harassed. Student organizations were surveilled. And all it took was one Facebook post to put someone on the radar. The line between online rumor and state suspicion disappeared completely ([SAGE Journals](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/full/10.1177/18681034231209504)).

The media, too, became fair game.
Duterte himself said, “Just because you’re a journalist, you’re not exempted from assassination.” And the DDS took that line and ran with it.

Maria Ressa was branded a foreign agent. Fact-checkers were accused of sabotage. Every attempt to correct a lie was met with a swarm of insults, threats, and organized smear campaigns ([Democratic Erosion](https://democratic-erosion.org/2022/05/14/rodrigo-dutertes-autocratic-regime-and-democratic-erosion-in-the-philippines/)).

Civil society—once vibrant, once fearless—started to pull back.
NGOs were painted as destabilizers.
Religious leaders were told to “mind their own pulpits.”
Student councils were red-tagged for holding forums.

And slowly, the silence spread—not out of apathy, but out of survival.
The DDS didn’t just promote a president. They **policed the nation’s conscience**.
They created a false binary: you’re either with the President, or you’re against the country.

There was no space for nuance. No room for honest dissent.
And that’s what frightened me the most.
Because they didn’t just silence critics—they made the whole country afraid to care.

## From Debate to Division – How DDS Cemented the Moral Chasm

There’s a quiet kind of damage that doesn’t leave blood on the streets.
It doesn’t trend. It doesn’t scream.
But it seeps into how we talk, how we relate, how we decide who to trust.
That’s the kind of damage the DDS continues to inflict.

Not just loyalty to Duterte.
But **division as a lifestyle**.

It started slowly.
Supporters began to say things like “DDS kami. Hindi kami duwag. Hindi kami elitista. Kami ang masa.” And if you weren’t DDS? You were immediately labeled as something else—“yellowtard,” “dilawan,” “elitista,” or worse, “kalaban ng bayan.”

There was no middle ground.
No space for nuance.
You were either **for Duterte**, or **anti-Filipino**.

That’s how they did it.
They didn’t just defend a president. They reshaped identity itself.
They made politics personal—deeply personal—and in doing so, they created a **moral chasm** so wide, it swallowed entire communities whole ([Democratic Erosion](https://democratic-erosion.org/2024/05/23/bridging-the-moral-chasm-understanding-the-increasing-polarization-in-the-philippines/)).

By 2022, the election wasn’t just the most contested in recent memory. It became the most **polarizing** political event in modern Philippine history ([UP CIDS](https://cids.up.edu.ph/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Polarization-in-the-2022-Philippine-Presidential-Election.pdf)).

People cut off friendships. Families stopped speaking to each other. Facts were no longer shared—they were **chosen**, depending on whose side you were on.

And even now, in 2025, long after Duterte stepped down, the aftershocks are still being felt.
His arrest didn’t close the divide. It deepened it.
Because those who were loyal to him aren’t retreating.
They’re doubling down.

And those who’ve been on the receiving end of DDS aggression? They’re tired. Angry. In no mood to reconcile.

And maybe that’s the real legacy of the DDS.
Not Duterte himself.
Not the drug war.
Not even the troll farms.
But the space they continue to shatter between truth and lies.
Between reason and rage.
Between Filipinos—and each other.

## Legacy and Reckoning – What We’ve Inherited, and What We’re Still Allowing

Duterte may be out of office.
But the machine he built? It's still running.
The language of cruelty.
The weaponization of loyalty.
The deep suspicion toward facts, journalists, institutions—even each other.

That didn’t end with his presidency. It didn’t end with his silence.
And it didn’t end when he was finally arrested.

In March 2025, the **International Criminal Court issued a warrant** for Rodrigo Duterte’s arrest, charging him with **crimes against humanity** linked to the War on Drugs ([BBC](https://www.bbc.com/news/world-36659258)).

It was a moment that should have marked closure.
But instead, it reignited the noise.
The DDS returned in full force—louder, more furious, more defiant. They flooded threads with claims of "persecution" and "foreign interference," defending their leader even as the evidence of thousands of deaths was laid bare ([Inquirer](https://newsinfo.inquirer.net/2043066/supporters-cry-persecution-injustice-over-arrest-of-duterte)).

And though the alliance that once held Duterte and Marcos together has quietly fractured, the culture they created—the "spectacle of the strongman"—continues to haunt us.

The DDS pages are still up.
The troll farms are still operational.
The distrust, still deep.
And the division, still raw.

But there’s another side, too.
Some former DDS are waking up.
Not with noise, but with silence. With reflection.
They’ve started to speak—not all at once, not always publicly, but it’s there: disillusionment. A realization that the "change" they were promised was just a different kind of decay ([PhilArchive](https://philarchive.org/archive/MAMTDK)).

Some say it was the broken promises.
Others say it was Duterte’s contradictions.
Maybe it was watching neighbors disappear. Or seeing how easily compassion became weakness in the new national vocabulary.

Whatever the reason, a few are stepping back.
But is that enough?
Is regret enough to rebuild what was broken?

Because we didn’t just lose truth.
We didn’t just weaken institutions.
We lost the ability to hear each other without hate in our throats.
We allowed cruelty to masquerade as leadership, and we let fanatics rewrite what it means to love our country.

And the worst part?
We’re still allowing it.
The DDS has held this country hostage for years—choking reason, silencing compassion, and framing violence as the only solution. And now, as the 2025 midterm elections approach, they are preparing for their next act.

They’ve already started.
They’re ensuring pro-Duterte senatoriables are seated—packing the upper house with loyalists, not legislators. They are turning the Senate into a **comedic clubhouse** where spectacle replaces service, and legislation is treated like a joke.

More damage.
More ruin.
More erosion of whatever dignity remains in our democracy.

It would be easy to say, *we need to do something.*
But what is that something?
What is that action—urgent, collective, deliberate—that doesn’t just resist the noise but begins to dismantle the machine?

Because reckoning isn’t just about punishing the powerful.
It’s about what we do now.
How we remember.
What we teach.
What we tolerate.

Duterte may be in custody.
But the noise he unleashed still lives in our culture, our platforms, our conversations.
And here’s the hard truth:
The reckoning of the DDS is terrifying—not because they don’t deserve it, but because **we’re all in the blast radius**.
They held the country hostage. And when karma finally arrives, it won’t just land on them.
The nation suffers with them.

Maybe we can’t undo the past.
But we can stop lying to ourselves about what it meant.
And if we’re brave enough—maybe we can start choosing something better.

## Conclusion: Back to the Table, Back to the Question

My coffee’s gone cold.
I hadn’t realized how long I’ve been sitting here, eyes bouncing between the mountain fog and the noise on my screen.

But the weight of this morning lingers.
Not in my chest. In my gut.
Because once again, I’m left with the same question:
What kind of country will my daughter inherit?

It’s not just about Duterte anymore. It’s not even just about the DDS.
It’s about what we’ve accepted. What we’ve normalized. What we continue to scroll past.

And here, in this strange stillness—this in-between space where the air is fresh but the news is toxic—I realize the most heartbreaking part isn’t the noise. It’s that **we’ve learned to live with it**. We’ve adjusted the volume, not our values.

I want to believe there’s still hope.
That there’s still time.
That the Philippines she’ll grow up in won’t be defined by the worst of who we’ve become, but by the quiet, stubborn refusal of a few to stop caring.

And maybe, that’s the start.
Not a rally. Not a speech. Not a thread that goes viral.
Just one father, finishing his coffee, deciding that silence isn’t safety—and that truth, no matter how inconvenient, is still worth writing down.

Congressman Arnan Panaligan and the Flood Control Controversy

He was mentioned in Senator Ping Lacson’s privilege speech. Not by name, but enough clues were there.
That got me curious.
On August 20, 2025, Lacson stood before the Senate and talked about billions poured into flood control projects in Oriental Mindoro. He didn’t spell out the lawmaker’s name, but his slide presentation did. The banners, the “AGILA” branding, and an accomplishment report covering June 30, 2022 to December 31, 2023 all pointed to one man: Oriental Mindoro 1st District Representative Arnan Capulong Panaligan.

## Who is Arnan Panaligan?
Panaligan isn’t new to politics. Born January 21, 1962 in Calapan, he studied at Holy Infant Academy, took up law at Ateneo de Manila, and passed the bar in 1987. He began his career as a Supreme Court researcher and later worked at the House of Representatives Electoral Tribunal.
His political track record is long. He served as mayor of Calapan from 1995 to 2004 and again from 2013 to 2022. He was governor of Oriental Mindoro from 2004 to 2010 after the death of Governor Bartolome Marasigan. In 2022, he became congressman for the province’s first district.
In 2021, he ranked third nationwide in mayoral job approval ratings, a recognition of his visibility and work. In Congress, he holds the post of House Deputy Majority Leader and was one of the eleven prosecutors in the impeachment of Vice President Sara Duterte in February 2025.
On paper, his career looks steady. Which makes Lacson’s allegations sting deeper.

## The Allegations
Lacson said Panaligan’s office was tied to billions in flood control projects. The numbers were staggering: ₱1.6 billion for Naujan, ₱807 million for Baco, and a combined total of more than ₱4.4 billion in just two municipalities.
The evidence Lacson showed wasn’t buried in government ledgers. It was on Facebook. Panaligan’s own accomplishment report carried the AGILA banner—Aksyon ng Gobyerno at Inisyatibo sa Larangang Lehislatura—and listed the projects as “funded by his office.” At project sites, physical banners carried the same claim of ownership.
Among the many projects, two stood out: the ₱95-million flood control structure in Barangay Burbuli, awarded to Silverwolves Construction Corporation, and the ₱231-million Butas River project in Naujan, awarded to the same firm.

## Why Lacson Called Some Projects “Unprogrammed”
The Facebook posts were one thing. They showed Panaligan publicly claiming the projects. But the funding path revealed a different story.
Lacson revealed that the Burbuli and Butas River projects were not part of the regular budget. They were funded through unprogrammed appropriations of the 2024 national budget.
Unprogrammed appropriations are standby funds. They aren’t guaranteed like programmed appropriations, which already have identified funding sources. They can only be released under specific conditions: when the government collects more revenue than expected, when new funding sources come in, when grants are secured, or when approved loans become available. DBM itself describes them as “standby authority” that requires certification before the money moves.
Accessing these funds is supposed to be complicated. Lacson described the process as masalimuot—long and difficult—yet here were two flood control projects from Panaligan’s district that had managed to secure them. As Lacson remarked: “Napakalakas naman po yata ni Congressman.”
The trail showed that these projects were not in the original National Expenditure Program submitted by the executive branch. They surfaced during budget deliberations in Congress. The House expanded the executive’s proposed ₱281.9 billion unprogrammed fund to over ₱531 billion in the 2024 General Appropriations Act.
In Barangay Mulawin alone, ₱1.9 billion was allocated for eight flood control projects. Only ₱810 million was in the original executive proposal. The remaining ₱1.1 billion appeared later in the House version.
This was what Lacson meant when he said projects “sprouted like mushrooms.” For him, insertions weren’t just generic entries in a spreadsheet—they had political owners. And when those owners put their names on banners and posts, they also took responsibility for how the money was spent.

## Evidence of Failures
Numbers alone don’t prove corruption. But the state of the projects spoke for themselves.
In August 2025, nine newly built flood control projects collapsed during the monsoon rains. Drone footage showed cracks and defects in structures that should have been new. In Barangay Mulawin, where ₱1.9 billion had been spent, portions of dikes were reportedly filled with sand instead of proper gravel and cement. Sheet piles were installed without concrete, making them unstable.
Some of the projects completed in February 2025 didn’t even last three months before giving way. Governor Humerlito “Bonz” Dolor named the contractors responsible, putting more pressure on DPWH and those tied to the contracts.
Patterns in the budget added to suspicions. Several projects carried identical contract amounts—₱77.2 million each—suggesting manipulation in pricing.

## Panaligan’s Defense
Faced with the allegations, Panaligan distanced himself from the failures. He said it was DPWH, not his office, that proposed and implemented the projects.
He pointed out that the projects had already been included in the National Expenditure Program when they reached Congress. Lawmakers approve budgets, but they don’t handle bidding, awarding of contracts, or project supervision. All of that, he stressed, is the responsibility of DPWH.
He added that the contractors were regional or national firms, not from Oriental Mindoro. And in June 2025, he had written to Public Works Secretary Manuel Bonoan, complaining about damaged flood control projects and demanding repairs.
To him, if the projects were anomalous, the fault lay with DPWH’s planning and oversight—not with him.

## Assessing the Case
Lacson’s evidence was hard to ignore. Banners, social media posts, project records, drone footage, and whistleblower accounts tied Panaligan’s name to the flood control works. The funding trail showed insertions and the unusual use of unprogrammed appropriations.
But there were gaps. Nothing in the documents proved that Panaligan personally profited. No record showed him meddling with the bidding or selecting contractors. His explanation of how the budget process works—and how the General Appropriations Act—was procedurally correct. And politicians taking credit for projects in their districts, even those they didn’t technically implement, is a common practice.
That leaves the case in a gray area. The evidence makes him politically accountable but doesn’t yet prove legal guilt.

## Institutional Actions
The issue didn’t end with Lacson’s speech. DPWH moved quickly, relieving engineers and staff tied to the Oriental Mindoro projects. Investigations into contractors began.
President Marcos also weighed in. He launched the sumbongsapangulo website, encouraging citizens to report anomalous projects. He noted the broader pattern of flood control contracting nationwide, suggesting that what happened in Oriental Mindoro wasn’t an isolated case but part of a deeper problem.

## The Bigger Picture
At its heart, this controversy isn’t just about one congressman. It exposes a system that allows politicians to claim ownership when things go right but escape accountability when they go wrong.
Congressional insertions make it possible to attach billions in projects during budget deliberations. Weak DPWH oversight lets substandard work pass. Contractor collusion keeps contract amounts suspiciously uniform. And the secrecy of the bicameral committee means no one really knows who inserted what into the budget.
In that kind of environment, Panaligan’s case becomes a symbol. He may or may not have broken the law, but his office ended up tied to projects that collapsed and billions that disappeared into concrete walls that couldn’t survive a single season of rain.

## Conclusion
The flood control projects in Oriental Mindoro were real. The money was real. So were the failures.
Panaligan’s name and office were publicly linked to them, through Facebook posts, accomplishment reports, and banners. But the funding mechanism—the use of unprogrammed appropriations—revealed how much of this controversy was rooted in a flawed system rather than one man alone.
So, was Lacson right to mention him in that privilege speech? Yes. Because even if the courts never find criminal liability, the public already saw where the money went and what little it achieved. And in politics, sometimes perception is enough to cast a shadow that no privilege speech can erase.

Fact-Checking Without Fact-Checkers: How Filipinos Can Verify Information Before Sharing

Discover essential fact-checking strategies to identify misinformation, counter DDS trolls, and avoid falling victim to online manipulation, deception, and propaganda.

I was halfway through my morning coffee when a memory hit me — a moment from years ago that still stings. 

As a freelance content writer, fact-checking has become second nature. But there was a time when I slipped. I once ghostwrote an article based on a source that looked credible but turned out to be biased. I didn’t realize it until after the piece was published. 

That mistake didn’t just change how I write — it changed how I consume information, especially on social media. 

Now, I pause before hitting ‘Share.’ I ask myself: 

Is this true? Where did this come from? What’s the motive behind this post? Who shared it? And more importantly, does it actually help anyone? 

These questions have become part of my routine because misinformation rarely comes from nowhere — there’s almost always a reason behind it. 

But what puzzles me is how many Filipinos — intelligent, thoughtful people — skip this step altogether. Some simply trust what they see online, while others want to fact-check but don’t know how. 

That’s why this article exists. 

If you’ve ever wondered how to verify information on your own — without relying on fact-checking organizations — this guide is for you.

## Why Fact-Checking Matters in the Philippines

The Philippines has been called “Patient Zero” in the global information disorder, having experienced large-scale political disinformation campaigns long before Brexit and the 2016 U.S. elections ([Fulcrum](https://fulcrum.sg/counter-disinformation-beyond-fact-checking-insights-from-the-philippines/)). 

Over the years, disinformation tactics have become more sophisticated. Fake social media personas, Instagram “thirst trap” influencers, and coordinated TikTok trends are now common strategies designed to manipulate public perception ([Media Support](https://www.mediasupport.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/Countering-disinformation-tools-and-initiatives-in-the-Philippines-May2023.pdf)). These methods play on emotions, encouraging people to share content impulsively — often without verifying its accuracy. 

Fact-checking groups like VERA Files, [Tsek.ph](http://tsek.ph/), and FactsFirstPH work hard to expose false claims. However, their capacity is limited. Misinformation spreads rapidly — often faster than these organizations can respond — especially during critical events like elections or national crises. 

That’s why learning to fact-check independently is more important than ever. Waiting for someone else to verify information isn’t always enough — sometimes, the responsibility falls on us.

## Why We Can’t Rely Solely on Fact-Checkers

Misinformation is like wildfire — it spreads fast, often faster than fact-checkers can respond. 

During major events like elections or national crises, false claims flood social media, drowning timelines in misleading content. By the time fact-checkers step in to debunk these claims, the damage is often done. People have already seen it, believed it, and in some cases, shared it. 

But misinformation doesn’t just spread randomly — it’s often pushed by coordinated troll networks that operate like well-oiled machines. 

These networks aren’t just noisy — they’re strategic. Troll farms work to dominate conversations and silence those who challenge false claims. 

They use several tactics:

Mass Posting: Trolls flood comment sections and forums with identical or similar posts, making fake narratives appear popular or widely accepted.

Attacking Dissenters: Anyone who dares to challenge false information — even with credible sources — risks being swarmed with insults, harassment, or threats. It’s a tactic designed to intimidate people into silence.

Amplifying Confusion: Trolls deliberately mix real facts with false claims, blurring the line between truth and deception. This makes it harder for the average person to tell what’s real and what’s manipulated.

Even when fact-checkers step in, these troll campaigns often succeed in keeping false narratives louder and more visible than the truth.

## Not All False Information Is the Same

To make things even more complicated, false information comes in different forms — and each type requires a different approach to verification.

Misinformation is false information shared without malicious intent — like when a friend unknowingly reposts a fake story, believing it’s true.

Disinformation is deliberately created to deceive — fabricated quotes, manipulated images, or staged videos designed to mislead.

Malinformation is real information twisted to cause harm — such as leaking private conversations out of context to damage someone’s reputation.

Each type demands attention — and relying on fact-checkers alone isn’t enough to stop it.

## The Risk of Blind Trust in Fact-Checkers

Even credible sources can make mistakes. 

I used to admire The Philippine Daily Inquirer. For years, they played a vital role in upholding democracy — challenging those in power, giving voice to the marginalized, and delivering news that mattered. 

But things changed. Ownership shifted, and with it came a noticeable shift in tone and coverage. What used to be a beacon of independent journalism began to feel different, and many long-time readers, including myself, began to question its editorial direction. 

That’s why blind trust — even in once-trusted names — is risky. Bias can slip in quietly, and if you’re not paying attention, you might find yourself consuming a version of the truth that’s been carefully curated.

## Taking Ownership of the Truth

While fact-checking organizations provide valuable insights, the responsibility doesn’t end with them. 

Learning to assess information on your own — asking Who shared this? What’s the motive? — is the best way to avoid being misled. Because in today’s digital landscape, misinformation isn’t just someone else’s problem. It’s ours. 

And the truth? That’s something worth protecting.

## How to Fact-Check Independently Before Sharing Information

Fact-checking doesn’t require special tools or professional training — it starts with asking the right questions and taking a moment to pause.

1. Ask Key Questions Before Sharing

Whenever you come across content that feels urgent, shocking, or emotionally charged, ask yourself:

Who shared this? Investigate the profile or page that posted the content. Is it a credible source, or does it have a history of spreading misleading information? Anonymous accounts and newly created profiles are common tools for spreading fake news.

What’s the motive? Consider why this content is being shared. Does it aim to inform, persuade, or provoke outrage? Emotional manipulation is a common tactic in disinformation campaigns.

Who benefits from this information? Ask yourself: Who gains if people believe this? Identifying who stands to profit — whether financially, politically, or socially — can reveal underlying agendas.

2. Verify the Source

Not all websites are trustworthy. Check if the information comes from a legitimate news outlet, government agency, or established organization. Be cautious with viral screenshots — they’re easily edited. If there’s no official link to support the claim, treat it as questionable.

3. Trace the Information to Its Origin

False information is often recycled or repackaged. One common tactic in the Philippines — particularly among DDS (Diehard Duterte Supporters) and their coordinated networks — is to take old photos or videos and re-contextualize them to suit a current narrative. 

For example, if they want to inflate the number of supporters at a rally, they might find a random video of a massive crowd from a concert or a religious event and claim it’s a political gathering. 

However, those who know how to investigate such content — by tracing the video’s origin or analyzing the metadata — can easily debunk these claims.

To verify visuals like these:

Reverse Image Search Tools:
[Google Images](https://images.google.com/) — Upload an image or paste its URL to find related images and their sources.
[TinEye](https://tineye.com/) — A powerful reverse image search tool that reveals where an image appears online.
[Yandex](https://yandex.com/images/) — Often better at identifying images that originate from non-Western sources.

Video Verification Tool:
[InVID](https://www.invid-project.eu/) — A browser extension designed to verify videos by identifying their origin, checking for manipulation, and providing context.

4. Cross-Check with Multiple Sources

If credible outlets aren’t covering the story, that’s a red flag. Look for multiple trustworthy sources reporting the same information. Be especially cautious if the content claims to reveal “shocking truths” that mainstream media is supposedly hiding. Reliable stories are usually reported across various outlets — even those with differing political views.

5. Check the Date and Context

Old news often resurfaces to manipulate public opinion. Confirm when the event occurred. Outdated stories are sometimes reshared to create confusion or stir emotions. Pay close attention to headlines — manipulative titles are often exaggerated or distort the actual content of the story.

6. Spot Emotional Manipulation

Content designed to trigger strong emotions — especially anger, fear, or outrage — often bypasses logical thinking. Trolls and disinformation campaigns use emotional language to push people into reacting impulsively. If a post feels like it’s demanding an immediate emotional response, take a step back and question why.

7. Consult Trusted Fact-Checking Tools as a Reference (Optional)

While this guide encourages independent verification, tools like [Snopes](https://www.snopes.com/), [FactCheck.org](http://factcheck.org/), and [Break the Fake Movement](https://breakthefakemovement.com/) can still provide useful insights as a starting point.

Fact-checking independently isn’t complicated — it’s about slowing down, asking the right questions, and refusing to let emotions drive your decisions. DDS trolls — and similar groups that rely on deception — thrive when people accept false information at face value. By taking these steps, you deny them the power to mislead you.

## How to Handle Misinformation Once You’ve Identified It

Spotting fake news is one thing — deciding what to do next is another. Correcting false information isn’t just about proving someone wrong. Done poorly, it can backfire, turning a simple correction into an argument or making someone feel embarrassed. Worse, if you engage trolls the wrong way, you risk giving their lies even more attention. 

So how do you handle misinformation without adding fuel to the fire?

## Don’t Feed the Trolls

Trolls thrive on conflict. They’re not interested in truth — their goal is to provoke you. The louder and angrier you get, the more they win. 

I’ve seen this play out many times. Someone posts fake content, a well-meaning person tries to correct it, and before long, the comment section turns into a shouting match. Meanwhile, the original post gains even more visibility — and the troll walks away satisfied. 

Instead of engaging directly, focus on informing others quietly and strategically. The best way to beat trolls is to deny them the attention they crave.

## Correct Misinformation Without Embarrassment

It’s different when someone you know — like a friend or relative — unknowingly shares something false. 

Calling them out in public can make them defensive. No one likes feeling embarrassed, even if they’re wrong. 

A better approach? Send a private message. Something like: 

“Hey, I saw your post earlier. I looked into it, and it seems like it’s not accurate. Here’s what I found.” 

This way, they won’t feel attacked — and they’ll be more open to listening. 

If you share proof, stick to credible links instead of just saying “That’s fake!” People are more willing to reconsider their stance when you guide them gently instead of shaming them.

## Add Context, Not Conflict

When misinformation goes viral, sometimes the best move is to add context rather than confront the person who shared it. A calm, fact-based comment can make a huge difference. 

Instead of arguing, try something like: “This has been debunked — here’s a link that explains it better.” Keep it short and to the point. Trolls will try to bait you — don’t fall for it.

## Report the Lies

If you come across fake content designed to deceive, reporting it can help slow its spread. Most social media platforms have tools for this:

On Facebook, click the three dots on the post and select “Find support or report post.”

On Twitter (X), tap the three dots and choose “Report Tweet.”

On TikTok, hold the video, tap “Report,” then select “Misinformation.”

On YouTube, click the three dots below the video and select “Report.”

It may seem small, but these reports add up. When enough users report false content, platforms are more likely to take action.

## Teach, Don’t Argue

Some people simply don’t know how to fact-check — and that’s where you can make a real difference. Instead of pointing fingers, try teaching people how to spot fake content for themselves. Share useful tools like [Google Images](https://images.google.com/) for reverse searches or [InVID](https://www.invid-project.eu/) to check suspicious videos. Sometimes guiding people to tools they can explore on their own is far more effective than telling them what to believe.

## Know When to Walk Away

Some people won’t change their minds, no matter how much evidence you provide. If you find yourself stuck in an endless debate, it’s better to walk away. Not everyone is ready to accept the truth, and wasting your energy on a lost cause only leads to frustration. 

Correcting misinformation isn’t about winning arguments — it’s about protecting the truth without adding to the noise. 

If you approach people with patience and respect, they’re more likely to listen. And if you find yourself dealing with someone who refuses to listen, remember: sometimes the strongest thing you can do is simply stop engaging.

## Conclusion: The Power of Independent Fact-Checking

Misinformation isn’t just an accident — it’s often part of a larger strategy to manipulate what people believe and how they act. Coordinated troll networks, emotionally charged posts, and cleverly disguised lies are all designed to divide us and obscure the truth. 

That’s why independent fact-checking is so important. It doesn’t take special skills or fancy tools — just curiosity, common sense, and a moment of pause. 

Asking simple questions like “Who posted this?” or “What’s the motive?” can prevent you from spreading false information — and from becoming part of the problem. 

You don’t have to win arguments with trolls or change the minds of people who refuse to listen. Sometimes, the most important work is simply ensuring that you — and those close to you — are informed by facts, not fiction. 

Every time you choose facts over fake news, you’re helping build a better-informed society — one that values the truth over convenience. 

The truth may not always travel fast — but it’s worth protecting.
279:
My bladder woke me up at 2 a.m., the way it always does. Normally, I’d slip back into sleep without much trouble, but this morning my eyes stayed open. I remember scrolling through a thread online where someone asked a simple but heavy question: “What tips or advice can you give someone with depression?”

I started reading the replies. A flood of generic answers poured in—half-serious, half-dismissive, and many that felt like they were written by someone who had never felt the weight of a single dark day.

Depression is not just sadness—it is a darkness that swallows everything. I remember days when even the thought of opening my eyes felt like a chore. And when you finally reach out, when you finally whisper, “I’m not okay,” you’re already breaking inside.

In the Philippines, that whisper often meets silence. Or worse, it meets words that sting more than they help. “Drama lang yan.” “Decide to be happy.” What should be an opening for compassion instead becomes another wound. And I know this not as an observer, but as someone who has lived through it.

## If I Were to Answer That Question

After going through the comments, I realized most weren’t helpful. But if I were to answer that question myself—based on science and my own struggle—here is what I would say:

**Start small, even if it feels pointless.**
Depression convinces you to stop moving, and the less you do, the heavier the days get. Science calls this behavioral activation, but what it really means is this: take one small step. Shower. Wash a plate. Step outside for air for five minutes. You’re not trying to fix your life; you’re just trying to prove to your body that you can still move.

**Write your thoughts down.**
Depression fills the mind with lies—“I’m worthless. Nothing will change.” Writing them on paper can take away some of their power. Psychologists call this cognitive restructuring. I just call it giving myself proof that my darkest thoughts aren’t the only truth.

**Practice presence, gently.**
Mindfulness is more than a trend or a hashtag. It’s the practice of bringing your mind back when it starts to spiral into the past or the future. Research shows it can actually change the way the brain responds to stress. Sit with your breathing, notice the sounds around you, or just feel the chair you’re sitting on. It doesn’t solve the problem, but it gives you a moment of peace.

**Lean on others, even in the smallest way.**
Isolation makes depression worse. Send one message. Ask a friend to sit with you, even in silence. Sometimes it’s not advice or solutions we need—it’s just the presence of another human being.

**Move your body, if you can.**
Not for fitness, not for goals. Just to feel alive. Walk, stretch, ride your bike, dance in your room. Physical activity releases chemicals that help regulate mood and improve sleep.

None of these erase depression. They’re not cures. But they’re anchors. Small, science-backed steps that keep you from drifting further out to sea. Because sometimes, all you need to get through the day is one gentle reminder: you are still here, and that’s enough for now.

## The Moment That Changed Everything: Resigning Due to Depression

Years ago, while working as a recruiter in a Philippine BPO, I hit my breaking point. Depression had turned my daily life into a battlefield. Every phone call felt like a mountain to climb. Every target I missed felt like a personal failure.

After months of pretending I was fine, I finally resigned. Not because I wanted to, but because I couldn’t survive another day in that seat.

I still remember that meeting with the company executives. I expected at least a word of compassion, or maybe an acknowledgment of the hard work I had put in before the darkness took over. Instead, I was met with blank stares. The conversation was cold, focused only on my resignation letter and the "abandonment" of my role.

That was the lesson. To them, I wasn’t an employee in pain. I was just someone who quit. And for many Filipinos, that is the reality—mental health is treated like a choice, and leaving because of it is seen as a weakness.

## Dismissed, Shamed, and Silenced: Filipino Responses to Depression

When I finally spoke about my depression, the replies I got weren’t comforting. They cut deeper than the illness itself.

**“Drama lang yan.”**
This one stings the most. It reduces depression to attention-seeking. As if fighting through the weight of existence is some sort of performance. What it really says is, “Stop bothering us with your pain.” And the person who dared to open up walks away thinking, “I should’ve kept quiet.”

**“Decide to be happy.”**
It sounds encouraging at first, but to someone drowning, it feels cruel. Happiness isn’t a switch you can just flip. If it were that easy, do you think I haven’t tried? Instead, I just fell quieter.

**“Just push forward… ignore it.”**
This is the resilience trap. Laban lang. Tuloy lang. Words we were all raised on. But depression isn’t something you can muscle through. It’s not a tired muscle; it’s a chemical and emotional storm. Telling someone to ignore it is like telling someone with a broken leg to just keep running.

**“Seek help.”**
This one sounds like the right advice, and it can be, but said carelessly it lands flat. When you’re at your lowest, even picking up the phone feels impossible. Simply telling someone to "seek help" without offering to help them find it or go with them can feel like another way of saying, “Handle this yourself.”

## Actual Harm: The Emotional Cost of Generic Replies

People don’t realize how fragile that moment is—the moment someone with depression opens up. It takes every ounce of courage they have left to say, “I’m not okay.” And yet, what they often get in return are words that feel like a slap.

Imagine someone drowning, waving frantically for help. You’re holding a lifebuoy, but instead of throwing it, you shout from the shore, “Have you tried not being underwater?” or “Just pray.”

Or picture someone bleeding on the floor. Instead of calling for help, you tell them, “Have you tried deciding not to be injured?”

That’s exactly how those generic lines sound to someone in depression. Empty. Dismissive. Cruel, even if you don't mean them to be.

I remember feeling smaller with every careless reply. The courage I had gathered to speak up was crushed, and I went back into my shell, certain that no one would ever understand. Telling someone to “cheer up” or “others have it worse” doesn’t just fail to help—it pushes us deeper into silence, and silence is where depression does its most dangerous work.

What a person in that darkness needs is not quick fixes or motivational speeches. They need presence. They need to know that their pain is real and that it’s okay not to have it all figured out yet. Sometimes, we need you to do something, not just say something. Because for someone who is barely hanging on, your words can either be a lifeline or the thing that finally lets go.

## Why Filipinos Respond This Way

I’ve asked myself many times why the usual replies to depression in the Philippines sound so dismissive. It’s not necessarily out of malice; it’s out of a culture that values resilience above all else.

We were raised to smile through pain, to keep family honor intact, and to avoid anything uncomfortable. It’s the “kaya mo yan” spirit we were all taught to carry.

This is what researchers now call toxic positivity—the idea that you should always look on the bright side, no matter how heavy life is. It shows up in our homes, our friendships, and especially in workplaces. In many companies, the focus is on productivity, and mental health is often seen as a hindrance to the bottom line.

The BPO industry, where I once worked, reveals how deep the problem runs. Research shows high rates of stress, anxiety, and depression among call center agents due to high-pressure environments, night shifts, and isolation. Even in online discussions, agents admit that depression feels almost “normal” in the industry, yet the support systems are often lacking.

And yet, when someone leaves work because of mental health, the response is often disbelief or indifference. There is still a stigma that says if you can’t “tough it out,” you’re not built for the job. But laws can only do so much. Culture is harder to change. Until we unlearn the instinct to dismiss pain, the silence will continue to grow.

## The Silent Crisis in Numbers

Sometimes people think depression is rare, or that it only happens to a few who “can’t handle life.” The numbers tell a different story.

In the Philippines, about 3.3 million people—3.34% of our population—live with depression. If you widen the scope to all mental health disorders, the number jumps to 12.5 million Filipinos, or 11.6% of the population.

What’s even more alarming is what’s happening to the young. Among Filipinos aged 15 to 24, reported depression rose from 9.6% in 2013 to 20.9% in 2021—that’s one out of every five young people. Many of them are still in school, still forming their futures, yet already carrying this weight.

The suicide numbers reveal the cost of neglect. In 2021, the suicide rate in the Philippines was 3.49 per 100,000 people. Between 2021 and 2022 alone, 404 students died by suicide. And in 2021, 7.5% of Filipino youth—around 1.5 million—attempted suicide.

Yet even with these numbers, the system meant to help is thin. There are fewer than one mental health worker for every 100,000 Filipinos, and government spending on mental health is just 0.22% of total health expenditures.

These numbers are not statistics to be rattled off—they are reminders of how urgent this crisis is. Behind each digit is a name, a face, a story. And too many of those stories end in silence, because the help came too late or never came at all.

## Creating Change: What Depression Truly Needs from Others

When someone is in the middle of depression, they don’t need empty slogans. They don’t need comparisons to people who have it “worse.” What they need is simple, but it requires genuine effort.

**Active, nonjudgmental listening:**
Sometimes the most healing thing you can do is simply sit with someone, let them speak, and resist the urge to offer advice or fix things immediately.

**Concrete help:**
Depression can strip away even the smallest energy needed to take care of yourself. That’s why real help looks like bringing someone a meal, helping them set up a doctor’s appointment, or just taking them out for a quiet walk.

**Validation:**
Sometimes all a person needs to hear is: “You’re not alone. I may not fully understand, but I’m here.” Validation reminds them that what they feel is real, and that it matters. It’s the opposite of the dismissive phrases we’re so used to.

Change doesn’t come from grand speeches. It starts in the way we respond to one person who opens up. It starts when we choose empathy over the easy, generic reply.

## What You Sound Like—From the Other Side

When you tell someone with depression to pray harder or to choose happiness, it doesn’t land the way you think it does. From the outside, it may sound like encouragement. From the inside, it sounds like an accusation. It sounds like you’re saying, “This is your fault.”

Depression isn’t a test of faith. It isn’t a failure of willpower. It is an illness that grips the mind and body. Telling a depressed person to just “be strong” or “decide to smile” is like telling someone drowning that they should’ve learned to breathe underwater.

People in depression carry a small, flickering lamp. It’s fragile, and every dismissive word is a gust of wind that threatens to blow it out. But when you respond with validation and presence, you’re not just listening—you’re shielding that flame.

## Healing Through Action: My Personal Road Forward

Looking back, I know I didn’t make it out of that darkness alone. Medication helped stabilize me, but it was the small, consistent actions that gave me back my life.

I found little lifelines outside the clinic too. Writing became one of them. Pouring my thoughts into pages like these allowed me to see the darkness for what it was—a part of my story, but not the whole book. I began to practice the very things I now share with others: staying present, moving my body even when I didn't want to, and finally letting people in.

That was when something shifted. Depression hadn’t disappeared, but I had new tools to face it. More importantly, I had stopped listening to the voices that told me to just “get over it.”

## Holding the Light for Someone Else

Filipinos are known for warmth, for community, for standing together when life gets heavy. But when it comes to the darkness of the mind, we have been standing apart for too long.

If we truly want to honor the values we’re proud of, then we need to start here—by learning to listen without judgment and by choosing compassion over the easy, harmful responses we’ve inherited.

I share this story not just to look back at my own struggle, but to remind anyone reading this: the way we respond to pain matters. For someone in the middle of depression, that presence can be the difference between despair and survival.
